Analyzing av-cli-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-u6hi4qka) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is safe to run with `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The file contains only standard package metadata and source definitions. The `source` arrays for each architecture point to versioned release binaries hosted on GitHub (github.com/aviator-co/av), which is the project's legitimate upstream. These are plain HTTP(S) downloads of pre-built archives, not git clones with possible executable hooks. There is no command substitution, `pkgver()` function (the version is static), or top-level code execution outside of simple variable assignments. All checksums are provided and static, with no use of 'SKIP'. No `prepare()`, `build()`, or `package()` functions are invoked during this phase, and in any case, the `package()` function is inert during `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`. Thus, sourcing this PKGBUILD and downloading the sources poses no risk of remote code execution or data exfiltration.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: no executable code on source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe: no executable code on source fetch[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is a standard binary packaging script for distributing a pre-built Go tool (`av-cli`) via the AUR. It fetches versioned, platform-specific tarballs from the official GitHub repository of the project (https://github.com/aviator-co/av), with verifiable `sha256sums` provided for both architectures. The sources are static release assets, not mutable branches, and the checksums match expected best practices for binary packages.

The `package()` function performs straightforward, safe operations: installing the binary to `/usr/bin`, placing the license in the proper directory, and copying man pages. All actions are confined to the package build directory (`${pkgdir}`) and follow standard Arch packaging conventions. No dynamic downloads, network calls, obfuscated code, or unexpected system modifications occur during installation.

Dependencies are minimal and reasonable: `git` is required for functionality, and `github-cli` is listed as an optional dependency for authentication. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, remote code execution, or supply-chain tampering. The package appears to be a clean, reproducible wrapper around an upstream binary release.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package with verified sources
</summary>
</security_assessment>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD only defines variables and a `package()` function. No top-level command substitutions, `pkgver()` function, or other code that executes during sourcing. The `source` arrays point to the official GitHub releases of `aviator-co/av`, which is the legitimate upstream. Checksums are provided and pinned. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will only download the tarballs and source the PKGBUILD, which contains no dangerous operations. The `package()` function is not executed during these commands.
</details>
<summary>Standard PKGBUILD with no dangerous top-level code.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<details>
The PKGBUILD is a standard GoReleaser-generated script that downloads pre-compiled binaries from the official `aviator-co/av` GitHub releases page. The source URLs use HTTPS and include SHA-256 checksums for integrity verification. The `package()` function performs normal installation of the binary, license, and man pages. There is no obfuscation, no unusual network requests, no execution of fetched code beyond normal installation commands, and no attempts to exfiltrate data or modify system files outside the package’s scope. The package is consistent with safe AUR practices.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR package; no suspicious code found.</summary>
</security_assessment>
